scenario: User Task in Tasklist. User clicks "claim." Wonders flow + unclaim option.
question: Tasklist claim / unclaim flow?

options.text: md:13-14 verbatim "User tasks can be automatically assigned to users and groups in the BPMN process, or they must be self-assigned from Tasklist. Once assigned to a user, the task can be completed. " :

Tasks can be automatically assigned if the BPMN element has an explicit assignee expression in zeebe:assignmentDefinition. Automatic assignment is a configurable capability, not a universal default. Many organizations require explicit self-assignment to distribute work and track accountability.

Once a User Task is claimed in Tasklist it can never be unclaimed or reassigned -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. Camunda 8 Tasklist explicitly supports unassigning a task, which releases it back to the candidate pool for another eligible user to claim. Administrators and authorized users can also reassign tasks via the API or UI. Unclaiming is a documented workflow step that enables task redistribution when the originally assigned user is unavailable.

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist claim flow: States: Available (Unclaimed): Listed in candidate group: members see in "Available Tasks". Anyone in group: can claim. Claimed: Assigned to specific user: that user's "My Tasks". Others can't access: until unclaimed. Completed: Task done: removed from active. Claim flow: ` Task created │ ├─ Assignee set explicitly → Claimed (assignee) │ └─ candidateGroups → Available │ ├─ User claims → Claimed (user) │ │ │ ├─ User completes → Completed │ │ │ └─ User unclaims → Available again │ └─ Timer / escalation → ... Claim API: Tasklist UI: "Claim" button. Tasklist API: explicit claim call. Unclaim: Release back to candidate pool. Useful if: user can't complete, going off duty. Auto-unclaim patterns: at end of day (custom). Reassign: Manager / admin can reassign. Override current claim. Use case: assignee unavailable. For tasks with explicit assignee: assignee` set in BPMN (per Set 14 Q22). Task created already claimed by that user. No further claim needed. For dynamic assignment: FEEL expression for assignee at task creation. Based on variable: dynamic. For multi-tasking: User can have multiple claimed: not exclusive. Limited by attention: not engine. For workload distribution: Round-robin (custom): rotate among candidates. Load-based: assign least busy. Skill-based: match expertise. For audit: Claim events: who, when. Unclaim events: tracked. Visible in audit log. For collaboration: Comments: per Tasklist version, on tasks. Sub-tasks: not standard BPMN. For mobile: Tasklist mobile: claim on go. Notifications: new available tasks. For SLA monitoring (per Set 16 Q52): Claim time: tracked. From assignment to claim: SLA1. From claim to complete: SLA2. For automation: Auto-claim via worker: User Task with no human interaction (verify pattern). System tasks typically Service Tasks; not User. For permission: Claim permission: typically same as task visibility. Reassign: typically admin. For Tasklist customisation: Custom UI: full flow control. Default Tasklist: standard flow. For batch claim: Multiple tasks at once (verify support). Useful for bulk processing. Anti-patterns: Claim and forget: blocking other candidates. No SLA: stale claims. No reassign mechanism: stuck if user absent. Best practices: Educate users on claim semantics. Monitor stale claims: aged + claimed. Auto-unclaim policies: at end of shift. Reassign admin powers: for outages. For the scenario (claim flow): User claims: task becomes theirs. Can unclaim: back to pool. Other candidates: re-eligible after unclaim. For testing: Test claim / unclaim: verify state transitions. Test permission: claim only if in group. For metrics: Claim time per task type: identify slow. Unclaim rate: high may indicate issues.

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
